message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-008: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly — never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str
File: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:85
Description: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by resolved conn_str with add-only lifecycle (never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN is the same. conn_str distinctness includes any URL variation (host alias, optional params, credential rotation that changes userinfo, sslmode toggles). A long-lived process targeting many distinct DSNs (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock per distinct string and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread per distinct string with no eviction — an unbounded cache and thread/connection leak. Credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, orphaning the old pool (atexit-closed but never reused/closed until exit).
PoC: Loop setting ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in ?application_name=run_i) and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; len(_CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(_POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and worker-thread count climbs by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.
Suggested fix: Canonicalize the cache key (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the key while keeping it for connect) so cosmetic/credential changes map to one entry; cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that pool.close()es and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS; prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll independently verify this finding by reading the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Upper bound (milliseconds) on how long the cross-process advisory lock may be waited for
56	# during schema bootstrap. A stuck/crashed peer that still holds a live PG session on the lock
57	# must NOT be able to hang bootstrap forever (#129); lock_timeout aborts the wait. 0 disables
58	# the bound (block indefinitely, the pre-#129 behavior). Read fresh on each bootstrap (#F-033) so
59	# an env var set after import still takes effect; the module constant is the default + the value
60	# tests monkeypatch.
61	_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS", "30000")))
62	
63	
64	def _migration_lock_timeout_ms() -> int:
65	    """The advisory-lock wait bound, re-read from the env each call (#F-033).
66	
67	    Falls back to the import-time module constant when the env var is unset, so a test that
68	    monkeypatches ``_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS`` still drives the value.
69	    """
70	    raw = os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS")
71	    if raw is None:
72	        return _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS
73	    return max(0, int(raw))
74	
75	
76	# Brief meta-lock guarding the process-local registries below (atexit registration, the open-pool
77	# list, the per-conn lock map, the pool cache). It is held ONLY for in-memory check-and-set — it
78	# is NEVER held across blocking cross-process I/O (pool.connection(), the advisory-lock wait, or
79	# setup() DDL). That blocking work is serialized per conn_str instead (#129), so a stuck advisory
80	# lock on one database cannot block initialization of an unrelated one.
81	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
82	
83	# Per-conn_str bootstrap locks: only same-conn_str initializers serialize; different databases
84	# bootstrap concurrently. Created lazily under _init_lock.
85	_CONN_INIT_LOCKS: dict[str, threading.Lock] = {}
86	
87	# Live pools cached by conn_str so the factory reuses one self-healing pool (and its worker
88	# thread) per database rather than opening a fresh pool on every call (#144).
89	_POOLS_BY_CONN: dict[str, Any] = {}
90	
91	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
92	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
93	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
94	_atexit_registered = False
95	
96	# Strips ``://user:password@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
97	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
98	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
99	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
100	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=...`` / ``pgpassword=...``),
101	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
102	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
103	
104	
105	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
107	
108	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
109	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
110	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
111	    """
112	    try:
113	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
114	        host = u.hostname or ""
115	        port = u.port
116	        scheme = u.scheme
117	        path = u.path
118	    except ValueError:
119	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
120	    if not host:
121	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
122	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
123	        host = f"[{host}]"
124	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
125	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
126	
127	
128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
141	    """
142	    if conn_str:
143	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
144	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
145	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
146	
147	
148	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
149	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
150	
151	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
152	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
153	    """
154	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
155	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
156	
157	
158	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
159	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
160	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
161	    that can raise)."""
162	    try:
163	        pool.close()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass
166	
167	
168	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
169	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
170	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
171	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
172	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
173	    with _init_lock:
174	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
175	    for p in pools:
176	        _safe_close(p)
177	
178	
179	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
180	    global _atexit_registered
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
183	        if not _atexit_registered:
184	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
185	            _atexit_registered = True
186	
187	
188	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
189	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
192	        if lock is None:
193	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
194	        return lock
195	
196	
197	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
198	    with _init_lock:
199	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
200	
201	
202	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
203	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
204	
205	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
206	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
207	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
208	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
209	    """
210	    timeout_ms = _migration_lock_timeout_ms()
211	    if timeout_ms:
212	        conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false)", (str(timeout_ms),))
213	    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
214	
215	
216	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
217	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
218	
219	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
220	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
221	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
222	    or the extra is not installed.
223	    """
224	    try:
225	        if use_memory:
226	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
227	
228	            return MemorySaver()
229	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
230	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
231	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
232	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
233	        raise RuntimeError(
234	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
235	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
236	        ) from exc
237	
238	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
239	
240	    # Fast path: reuse a live, self-healing pool for this conn_str rather than opening a new pool
241	    # (and its background worker thread) on every call (#144). The liveness check on checkout
242	    # replaces dead connections, so a cached pool stays valid across transient outages.
243	    cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)
244	    if cached is not None:
245	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
246	
247	    # Serialize bootstrap PER conn_str — NOT process-globally (#129). Unrelated databases
248	    # initialize concurrently, and a stuck advisory lock on one conn_str can never block init of
249	    # another. The brief meta-lock (_init_lock) is only taken inside the helpers, never held
250	    # across the blocking advisory-lock wait / DDL below.
251	    with _conn_init_lock(conn_str):
252	        cached = _cached_pool(conn_str)  # another thread may have built it while we waited
253	        if cached is not None:
254	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, cached))
255	
256	        failure: str | None = None
257	        pool: Any = None
258	        try:
259	            # Pool bounds are env-tunable (#F-040): the reasoning seam already runs 8 worker
260	            # threads, so a fixed max_size=8 can be saturated under concurrent reasoning+checkpoint
261	            # load on larger hardware. connect_timeout bounds an individual TCP connect so a
262	            # black-holed host fails fast instead of hanging bootstrap for the OS default.
263	            min_size = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE", "1")))
264	            max_size = max(min_size or 1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_MAX_SIZE", "8")))
265	            connect_timeout = max(1, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_POOL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT", "10")))
266	            pool = ConnectionPool(
267	                conn_str,
268	                min_size=min_size,
269	                max_size=max_size,
270	                kwargs={
271	                    "autocommit": True,
272	                    "prepare_threshold": 0,
273	                    "row_factory": dict_row,
274	                    "connect_timeout": connect_timeout,
275	                },
276	                check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
277	                open=True,
278	            )
279	            _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
280	            # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
281	            # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
282	            # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
283	            with pool.connection() as conn:
284	                _acquire_migration_lock(conn)  # bounded wait so a stuck peer can't hang us (#129)
285	                try:
286	                    # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
287	                    # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
288	                    # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
289	                    PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
290	                finally:
291	                    # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
292	                    # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
293	                    try:
294	                        conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
295	                    except Exception:
296	                        pass
297	            with _init_lock:
298	                _POOLS_BY_CONN[conn_str] = pool  # cache for reuse on later calls (#144)
299	            return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
300	        except Exception as exc:
301	            # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
302	            # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
303	            # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
304	            failure = (
305	                f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
306	                f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
307	                "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
308	            )
309	            # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
310	            # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
311	            if pool is not None:
312	                _safe_close(pool)
313	                with _init_lock:
314	                    if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
315	                        _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
316	        raise RuntimeError(failure)
317	
318	
319	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
320	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
321	
322	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
323	    otherwise Postgres.
324	    """
325	    if use_memory is None:
326	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
327	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
328

content.text:
The code confirms the structural facts of the finding. Let me verify the key claims:

1. `_CONN_INIT_LOCKS` (line 85) — keyed by `conn_str`, add-only via `_conn_init_lock` (lines 188-194). Never pruned. **Confirmed.**
2. `_POOLS_BY_CONN` (line 89) — keyed by `conn_str`, add-only at line 298. Never evicted. **Confirmed.**
3. `conn_str` comes directly from `get_postgres_connection_string()` → `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` env, no canonicalization. So `?application_name=run_i` variants are distinct keys. **Confirmed.**
4. Each new pool spawns a worker thread (ConnectionPool background worker) and is registered in `_OPEN_POOLS`, closed only at atexit. **Confirmed.**

Let me check how loopback enforcement works and whether multi-tenant/rotating-DSN usage is plausible in this codebase, and whether there's any pruning I'm missing.

content.content:

src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:89:_POOLS_BY_CONN: dict[str, Any] = {}
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:149:    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:154:    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:191:        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:193:            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:199:        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:216:def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:298:                _POOLS_BY_CONN[conn_str] = pool  # cache for reuse on later calls (#144)
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:307:                "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:319:def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:327:    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py:16:    configured_checkpointer,
src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py:17:    get_checkpointer,

content.text: The mechanics of the finding are accurate. Now the key question for production-readiness: is this a *genuine concern* in this codebase, or speculative? The finding hinges on a long-lived process targeting *many distinct DSNs*. Let me check the deployment model — is this a single-tenant single-DSN on-box product, or multi-tenant?

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rn --include="*.py" -e "setenv(\"ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL\|os.environ\[.ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL" src 2>/dev/null; echo "---tenant/multi---"; grep -rln --include="*.py" -ie "multi.tenant\|tenant" src 2>/dev/null | head; echo "---how callers use it---"; grep -rn --include="*.py" -e "configured_checkpointer\|get_checkpointer" src | grep -v "persistence/"

content.text:
No callers in `src` outside the persistence package itself, no multi-tenant model, and nothing in the codebase ever mutates `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` at runtime (only tests do, via monkeypatch). The deployment model per ADR-0003 and the module docstring is explicitly **on-box, single logical store** ("the store is on-box only"; default `postgresql://localhost:5432/assay`).

Let me check the loopback enforcement, since that bounds how many distinct DSNs are even reachable.

content.content:
1	"""Data-sovereignty helper: enforce that network endpoints are loopback-only (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every engine component that takes a host/URL for an on-box service (local model runtime,
4	tracing collector, vector store, checkpoint database) routes it through here so a non-local
5	address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import ipaddress
11	import re
12	from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse
13	
14	# A libpq keyword/value token: ``key = value`` with optional whitespace around ``=`` (libpq
15	# permits it) and an optionally single/double-quoted value (which may contain spaces).
16	_DSN_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"(\w+)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
17	
18	
19	class NonLocalEndpointError(RuntimeError):
20	    """Raised when a configured endpoint is not loopback (would leave the machine)."""
21	
22	
23	def is_loopback_host(host: str | None) -> bool:
24	    """True only for genuine loopback hosts.
25	
26	    Parses the host as an IP and tests the loopback network (127.0.0.0/8, ::1), so a public
27	    name that merely *starts with* ``127.`` (e.g. ``127.0.0.1.evil.com``) is correctly
28	    rejected. The literal ``localhost`` is the only accepted non-IP host.
29	    """
30	    if not host:
31	        return False
32	    h = host.strip().lower()
33	    if h == "localhost":
34	        return True
35	    try:
36	        return ipaddress.ip_address(h.strip("[]")).is_loopback
37	    except ValueError:
38	        return False
39	
40	
41	def require_loopback_url(url: str, *, what: str) -> str:
42	    """Return ``url`` if its host is loopback; else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`."""
43	    host = urlparse(url).hostname
44	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
45	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
46	            f"{what} must be loopback-only (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got host {host!r} "
47	            f"from {url!r}"
48	        )
49	    return url
50	
51	
52	def require_loopback_host(host: str, *, what: str) -> str:
53	    """Return ``host`` if it is loopback; else raise. (``0.0.0.0`` binds all interfaces and
54	    is correctly rejected — it would expose data off-box.)"""
55	    if not is_loopback_host(host):
56	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
57	            f"{what} must be a loopback host (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); got {host!r}"
58	        )
59	    return host
60	
61	
62	def _authority_hosts(netloc: str) -> list[str]:
63	    """Every host in a URI authority, supporting libpq's comma-separated multi-host form.
64	
65	    ``urlparse().hostname`` returns only the *first* host of a multi-host authority
66	    (``host1:port1,host2:port2``), so a remote second host would slip past a single-host
67	    check (audit #D3). Each host may carry its own ``:port`` and IPv6 hosts are bracketed.
68	
69	    Userinfo is stripped at the **first** ``@`` — matching libpq, which (unlike ``urlparse``,
70	    which uses the last ``@``) treats the first ``@`` as the userinfo delimiter. A string like
71	    ``[REDACTED]:5432@localhost`` is therefore read as host ``evil.com`` here, not
72	    ``localhost`` (audit #D5). Any host element still containing ``@`` after that is ambiguous
73	    and is returned verbatim so the caller's loopback check fails it closed.
74	    """
75	    if "@" in netloc:
76	        netloc = netloc.split("@", 1)[1]
77	    hosts: list[str] = []
78	    for part in netloc.split(","):
79	        p = part.strip()
80	        if not p:
81	            continue
82	        if p.startswith("["):  # [ipv6] or [ipv6]:port
83	            end = p.find("]")
84	            hosts.append(p[1:end] if end != -1 else p[1:])
85	        else:
86	            hosts.append(p.rsplit(":", 1)[0] if ":" in p else p)
87	    return hosts
88	
89	
90	def require_local_uri(uri: str, *, what: str) -> str:
91	    """Return ``uri`` if it is local (ADR-0003), else raise :class:`NonLocalEndpointError`.
92	
93	    Three forms are recognized, validated against what the real client (libpq for postgres,
94	    urllib for http, sqlite for files) actually connects to — not merely what ``urlparse``
95	    reports, since the two can disagree:
96	
97	    - **URI with an authority** (``scheme://host…`` or scheme-relative ``//host…``): *every*
98	      host in the authority must be loopback (incl. each of a comma-separated multi-host
99	      authority, #D3), AND any ``host``/``hostaddr`` query parameter that libpq would use to
100	      override the authority host (#D2). ``hostaddr`` is checked independently of ``host``.
101	    - **libpq keyword/value DSN** (no authority but ``key=value`` tokens): ``host``/``hostaddr``
102	      must be loopback, tolerating libpq's whitespace/quoting around ``=`` (#P1, #D1).
103	    - **bare path** (no authority, no ``=``): a local file/dir — but a Windows UNC path
104	      (``\\\\server\\share``) is rejected as remote.
105	
106	    Inputs that cannot be parsed safely fail closed (rejected), never accepted.
107	    """
108	    try:
109	        parsed = urlparse(uri)
110	        netloc = parsed.netloc
111	        query = parsed.query
112	    except ValueError:
113	        # e.g. a malformed IPv6 authority — we cannot determine the host, so refuse.
114	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
115	            f"{what}: endpoint could not be parsed safely; rejecting (ADR-0003)"
116	        ) from None
117	
118	    if netloc:
119	        # A backslash never legitimately appears in a URI authority; a WHATWG-normalizing
120	        # client (e.g. some HTTP stacks) treats '\' as '/', so it could read a different host
121	        # than urlparse. Refuse rather than risk that differential (audit #D6, defense-in-depth).
122	        if "\\" in netloc:
123	            raise NonLocalEndpointError(
124	                f"{what}: backslash in URI authority is not permitted (ADR-0003); rejecting"
125	            )
126	        for h in _authority_hosts(netloc):
127	            # A residual '@' means the authority is ambiguous between this parser and libpq —
128	            # fail closed (audit #D5).
129	            if "@" in h or not is_loopback_host(h):
130	                raise NonLocalEndpointError(
131	                    f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); authority "
132	                    f"names non-loopback host {h!r}"
133	                )
134	        for key in ("host", "hostaddr"):
135	            for val in parse_qs(query).get(key, []):
136	                h = val.strip()
137	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
138	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
139	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); URI query "
140	                        f"{key}={h!r} points off-box"
141	                    )
142	        return uri
143	
144	    if "=" in uri:  # libpq keyword/value DSN (no URI authority)
145	        for match in _DSN_TOKEN_RE.finditer(uri):
146	            if match.group(1).strip().lower() in {"host", "hostaddr"}:
147	                h = match.group(2).strip().strip("'\"")
148	                if h and not is_loopback_host(h):
149	                    raise NonLocalEndpointError(
150	                        f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); DSN names "
151	                        f"non-loopback host {h!r}"
152	                    )
153	        return uri
154	
155	    stripped = uri.lstrip()
156	    # Windows treats ANY two leading separators (in any mix of '\' and '/') as a UNC prefix
157	    # to a remote SMB share — \\srv, //srv, /\srv, \/srv all resolve off-box (audit #D6).
158	    # (The pure '//host' form is already caught by the authority branch above; covering it
159	    # here too keeps the branch self-contained.)
160	    if len(stripped) >= 2 and stripped[0] in "\\/" and stripped[1] in "\\/":
161	        raise NonLocalEndpointError(
162	            f"{what} must be a local store (ADR-0003 data sovereignty); UNC-style path "
163	            f"{uri!r} is a remote share"
164	        )
165	    return uri  # bare path / dir store
166

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Let me weigh the verdict.

**The structural facts are all true:**
- `_CONN_INIT_LOCKS` and `_POOLS_BY_CONN` are process-lifetime, add-only, keyed by the raw `conn_str` with no canonicalization, no eviction, no LRU cap.
- `conn_str` distinctness includes any cosmetic URL variation (`?application_name=...`, sslmode, etc.) — these are not stripped before keying.
- Each distinct `conn_str` builds a new `ConnectionPool` (background worker thread) registered in `_OPEN_POOLS`, closed only at `atexit`.
- The PoC (looping `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` over N distinct loopback DSNs) would indeed grow both dicts to N and add a worker thread per DSN, none reclaimed before exit.

**But the production-readiness severity is bounded heavily:**
- Loopback enforcement (`require_local_uri`) restricts every DSN to `localhost`/`127.0.0.0/8`/`::1`. There is no multi-tenant model in this codebase — no `tenant` references, no remote DSNs, no runtime code path that mutates `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` (only test monkeypatching does).
- The documented deployment model (ADR-0003, module docstring, `_DEFAULT_PG`) is a single on-box logical store. In normal operation `conn_str` is constant, so both dicts hold exactly one entry — the `#144` cache fast-path is the whole point.
- The "credential rotation" / "multi-tenant runner" / "test harness rotating DSNs" scenarios are the only triggers, and none of them exist as a real code path here. Credential rotation on a loopback single-store deployment is uncommon and even then accumulates at most a handful of entries over the process lifetime — not an operational concern, and the orphaned pools are atexit-closed.

So: the *mechanism* is real and reproducible exactly as the PoC describes, but for *this* product (single on-box store, loopback-enforced, no multi-tenancy, no runtime DSN mutation) it is a latent/hardening nicety rather than a genuine production failure mode. It is essentially speculative against the actual deployment model. The growth is theoretically unbounded but practically bounded to ~1 entry in real use. This is a real but low-severity hardening improvement, not a production-readiness defect.

Given the instruction to confirm only if it is a genuine concern in current code and not speculative, the trigger conditions are speculative for this codebase. I'll confirm the code property is accurately described but assess it as low severity / not a true production problem.

input.reasoning:
The structural code facts in the finding are accurate: in src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py, _CONN_INIT_LOCKS (line 85, populated at 191-193) and _POOLS_BY_CONN (line 89, populated at 298) are process-lifetime, add-only dicts keyed by the raw resolved conn_str with NO canonicalization, NO LRU cap, and NO eviction. conn_str comes verbatim from get_postgres_connection_string() (line 154, ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL env) so cosmetic variants (?application_name=..., sslmode toggles, rotated userinfo) are distinct keys. Each distinct key builds a new psycopg_pool.ConnectionPool (its own background worker thread, lines 266-278), registered in _OPEN_POOLS and closed only at atexit (179-185, 168-176). The PoC is mechanically reproducible exactly as written.

However, it is NOT a genuine production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code for THIS product, and the triggering scenarios are speculative against the actual deployment model:

1. Every conn_str is forced through require_local_uri (line 155 -> src/assay_engine/_local.py:90), which rejects any non-loopback host. There is no multi-tenant/remote fan-out capability.
2. There is no multi-tenant model anywhere (grep for "tenant" in src returns nothing) and NO runtime code path that mutates ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL — only tests monkeypatch it. No caller in src outside the persistence package even invokes get_checkpointer/configured_checkpointer.
3. The documented and enforced deployment model (ADR-0003, module docstring lines 4-6, _DEFAULT_PG line 48 "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay") is a single on-box logical store. In normal operation conn_str is constant, so both dicts hold exactly ONE entry — that single-entry cache is the entire purpose of the #144 fast path (lines 240-245).

The "multi-tenant runner", "test harness rotating DSNs", and "credential rotation" triggers cited by the finding are not real code paths in this repo; they are hypothetical. Even under rare loopback credential rotation, growth is a handful of entries over a long process life with orphaned pools atexit-closed — not an operational leak. The "unbounded" growth is unbounded only in theory; in practice it is bounded to ~1. This is at most a low-value defense-in-depth/hardening nicety (canonicalizing the key, adding an LRU), not a production defect. Per the instruction to confirm only genuine, non-speculative concerns in current code, this does not meet the bar.

content.text:
I've completed the verification and returned my assessment via StructuredOutput.

Summary: Finding G-008 is **not confirmed** as a true production-readiness problem. The code facts are accurate (`_CONN_INIT_LOCKS` and `_POOLS_BY_CONN` at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:85` and `:89` are add-only, unbounded, keyed by raw uncanonicalized `conn_str`, and the PoC is reproducible), but the triggering scenarios are speculative for this codebase: loopback enforcement via `require_local_uri` (`/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py:90`), no multi-tenant model, no runtime path mutating `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` (only tests do), and a documented single on-box store model mean both dicts hold ~1 entry in real use. Severity: low — a defense-in-depth nicety, not a production defect.
